coredata.dc:title: Aggregation-induced bimodal excitation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of new coccine and solid-state multicolor lighting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Guoqiang
Li, Tingting
Wang, Yiru
Hu, Houwen
Xing, Haoming
Tang, Siyuan
Gao, Sineng
Leng, Xuan
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Trace detection of foodstuff pigments have gained increasing attention because of their close association with biological and environmental processes. Herein, we propose an innovative bimodal excitation nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for ratiometric sensing of new coccine (NC) pigment, which are synthesized by using melamine and o-phenylenediamine as precursors via solvothermal treatment. With the increase of the N-CDs concentration, N-CDs exhibit not only a concentration-dependent tunable color behavior, but also a novel aggregation-induced bimodal excitation phenomenon. Considering this distinctive bimodal excitation behavior, a ratiometric sensor based on N-CDs has been developed for the detection of the NC in different organic solvents due to the inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The intensity ratio of two excitation signals is linear with the NC concentration in the range of 0.032–100 µM, and the limit of detection is as low as 32.1 nM. Meanwhile, we realize the design of multicolor-emission N-CDs/polymer films. All in all, this work presents a novel kind view of the mechanism of distinctive bimodal excitation of N-CDs, and further proposes an innovative ratiometric method for the screening analysis of NC in food samples and environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
Bimodal excitation fluorescence
Ratiometric sensor
New coccine detection
Fluorescent film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Aggregation-induced emission and excitation behavior of N-CDs
Selectivity experiment and detection of NC
Determination of NC in real samples
Multicolor film based on aggregation-induced multicolor of N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM and high-resolution TEM images of as-synthesized N-CDs. (b) Particle size distribution of N-CDs measured by TEM. (c) AFM image of N-CDs. Inset: the height profile generated from the white dotted lines in the AFM image. (d) XRD pattern of N-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs (green line), OPD (red line) and melamine (purple line). (f) Full XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs, with high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (g), N 1 s (h) and O 1 s (i). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of N-CDs. The insets show the optical images of N-CDs solution under daylight (left) and a 365 nm illumination (right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Excitation-emission matrix graph map for N-CDs. The normalized fluorescence intensity of N-CDs under (d) ultraviolet irradiation for different timescales (150 W and 365 nm mercury lamp), (e) visible light irradiation for different timescales and (f) different ionic strengths of NaCl solution.

(a) Normalized PL spectra of the N-CDs under an optimal excitation wavelength. (b) The PL emission wavelength and intensity of N-CDs along with different concentrations. (c) Tauc-plot for bandgap calculation in solutions with different concentrations of N-CDs. (d-i) 2D fluorescence maps of N-CDs aqueous solution with different concentrations.

(a) PLE spectra of N-CDs with different concentrations. (b) PL decay curves of N-CDs under different excitations. (c) PL decay curves and fitting by dual-exponential function profiles of N-CDs at different concentrations under the same excitation wavelength. (d) Mechanism illustration of aggregation-induced bimodal excitation phenomenon.

(a) PLE spectrum of N-CDs solution with different concentrations of NC. (b) The corresponding relationship between I459/I401 and the NC concentration. (c) Images of N-CDs with different concentrations of NC under visible light and UV light. (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs (curve i), NC (curve ii), N-CDs/NC (curve iii) and fitting curve (curve iv). (e) The UV–vis absorption of NC, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-CDs. (f) PL decay profiles of N-CDs and N-CDs/NC. (g) Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism for the detection of NC.

(a) Fluorescent film preparation flow chart. (b) Photographs of fluorescent films with different N-CDs content under 365 UV light irradiation (the content increases from left to right). (c) The normalized optimal fluorescence spectrum of films. (d) The UV absorption spectrum of films. (e) Tauc-Plot for bandgap calculation in films with different N-CDs content.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis, novel fluorescence characteristics and multiple applications of N-CDs.
Determination of NC in real samples using fluorescence and HPLC techniques.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The critical t-value at the 95% confidence level is 2.78.

head.ce:title: Aggregation-induced bimodal excitation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of new coccine and solid-state multicolor lighting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG)
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace detection of foodstuff pigments have gained increasing attention because of their close association with biological and environmental processes. Herein, we propose an innovative bimodal excitation nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for ratiometric sensing of new coccine (NC) pigment, which are synthesized by using melamine and o-phenylenediamine as precursors via solvothermal treatment. With the increase of the N-CDs concentration, N-CDs exhibit not only a concentration-dependent tunable color behavior, but also a novel aggregation-induced bimodal excitation phenomenon. Considering this distinctive bimodal excitation behavior, a ratiometric sensor based on N-CDs has been developed for the detection of the NC in different organic solvents due to the inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The intensity ratio of two excitation signals is linear with the NC concentration in the range of 0.032–100 µM, and the limit of detection is as low as 32.1 nM. Meanwhile, we realize the design of multicolor-emission N-CDs/polymer films. All in all, this work presents a novel kind view of the mechanism of distinctive bimodal excitation of N-CDs, and further proposes an innovative ratiometric method for the screening analysis of NC in food samples and environmental pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodstuff color is an essential index in the sensory quality of food. Thus, pigments are generally applied to variegate foodstuff for improving sensory properties or camouflaging discoloration during food preservation. New coccine (NC) is one of the most widely used azo synthetic pigments and appears to be red in the form of powder [1]. According to previous reports, NC is of considerable genotoxic potential, as a partial intercalator, targeting DNA and ultimately causing chain breaks. Therefore, extensive or long-term exposure to NC will definitely be detrimental to organisms [2,3]. With these in mind, NC is authorized as an additive and has a strict level for the use in China, whereas it is forbidden in the United States, Canada and numerous other countries [4]. Although different countries have strictly stipulated the permitted levels of NC in food products [5], the addition of NC by manufacturers to beautify food appearance is still inevitable. Therefore, a reliable and convenient NC detection method is crucial to ensure food safety and human health.

To date, methods for detecting NC rely on the laboratory instrumental analysis, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), high-performance ion chromatography (HPIC) and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [6–10]. However, most of them inevitably involve some deficiencies, such as elaborate sample preparation procedures, costly instrumentation, prolonged analysis time and skilled personnel, which significantly limit their application scope and working efficiencies. In contrast, fluorescence sensors, such as fluorescence dyes, tombarthite materials and carbon nanomaterials, have been widely used in chemical and biochemical analysis because of their superior sensitivity, excellent accuracy and rapid response [11,12]. Among these materials, carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have captured tremendous attentions due to their excellent photostability, low cell toxicity and superior biocompatibility [13,14]. Recently, some fluorometry methods based on carbon nanomaterials have been employed for sensing NC. For example, Liu et al. developed nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs for the selective and sensitive determination of NC by detecting bright blue mono emission signal at 448 nm [15]. Zhang et al. prepared low-toxic GQDs to evaluate the concentration of NC, which has an excellent linear relationship with the signal intensity of blue emission [16]. Nevertheless, most of them are based on mono-component signal variations, which may cause a large margin of error due to the interference of the external environment and instruments. To solve this problem, it is exceptionally desirable to develop fluorescent nanoprobes with ratiometric fluorescence sensing capability. Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes enable to provide an environmental interference correction and exclude the fluctuation of light excitation intensity. Commonly, two fluorescence signals at different wavelengths are simultaneously measured and the intensity ratio is used for the quantitative analysis. For instance, Dai et al. fabricated green/red dual-emission CDs as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor to determine NC through a robust inner filter effect [17]. Wang et al. constructed a nano-sensor with gadolinium-doped CDs for the morin detection based on ratiometric fluorometry and colorimetry dual-mode [18]. It is obvious that these ratiometric fluorescence sensors are constructed to detect analytes based on their emission signals. However, the analysis strategy relying on fluorescence excitation information for NC detection has never been reported. Thus, it is still revealing and challengeable to develop a ratiometric fluorescence method with multiple excitation signals to monitor NC.

In our work, an innovative ratiometric method based on bimodal excitation signals of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is developed for the trace amount detection of NC. As shown in Scheme 1 , N-CDs are synthesized by a straightforward one-step solvothermal method using melamine and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. With the concentration of N-CDs increasing, the as-prepared N-CDs exhibit a concentration-dependent tunable color phenomenon. Simultaneously, it shows an aggregation-induced bimodal excitation behavior in the specific concentration range. Such behavior originates from the kinetic process of electrons and the discrepancy of electric potential in N-CDs. Upon the addition of NC, the excitation peak at 459 nm declines faster than that at 401 nm due to the energy transfer system between N-CDs and NC (the inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)). Therefore, a dual-excitation ratiometric sensor for NC detection is developed based on N-CDs and represents an excellent linear regression in the concentration range from 0.032 to 100 µM. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as 32.1 nM. In addition, based on the concentration-dependent tunable color phenomenon, we realize the preparation of multicolor-emission N-CDs/polymer films by integrating N-CDs of different concentrations with the polymer matrices. Inspired by this work, this innovative analysis strategy widens the road for the construction method of ratiometric fluorescence sensor and provides a new view of the novel bimodal excitation behavior origin.

All chemical reagents were purchased with analytical grade requiring no additional purification. O-phenylenediamine (OPD, 99.5%), melamine (99%), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5%), ethanol (≥99.8%), NC (≥99%), Rhodamine 6G (≥99.0%), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, ≥99%) and other chemicals were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Deionized water throughout all experiments was obtained from the Millipore system. The energy drink, tea and wine were bought from the local market around Ningbo University (Ningbo, China).

N-CDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method. 1 mmol melamine and 1 mmol OPD were dissolved in 20 mL DMF with ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and kept at 150 °C for 8 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the obtained solution was purified via a 0.22 µm filter to remove large particles and unreacted substances. Finally, the purified solution was evaporated in vacuum for 24 h and then stored at 4 °C for further use.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, Japan Electron) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Cypher S, Oxford Instruments) were used to characterize morphologies and the particle size of the N-CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured by an X-ray source with a PANalytical X’Pert PRO diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of N-CDs were obtained with a spectrometer (Bruker, WQF-410). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was performed on a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer. Raman spectrum was collected by a HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer at an excitation of 532 nm. The ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis) spectra were performed by a UV-5800 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) at room temperature. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were carried out using the FL3-111 spectrometer (Horiba, France) at indoor temperature. The determination of NC verified by HPLC was carried out on an Agilent HPLC 1200 series (USA) with a photodiode array detector (508 nm).

The fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of the N-CDs was generally measured according to the previous reports. Especially, Rhodamine 6G (FQY = 95% in ethanol) was selected as the reference. At the same excitation wavelength, a series of N-CDs solutions with different concentrations and reference substances were prepared to keep all corresponding absorbance of UV–vis within 0–0.1. The FQY of fluorescent substance was calculated according to the following equation [19]:
(1)
FQY =
Q
ST
×
K X
K
ST
×
η X
η
ST
2
where QST is the fluorescence quantum yield of Rhodamine 6G in ethanol, K corresponds to the slope of integrated emission intensity against absorbance, and η is the refractive index (1.44 for DMF and 1.36 for ethanol). The subscript “ST” refers to the reference substance, and “X” refers to the fluorescent substance.

The detection of NC was performed with N-CDs at room temperature in organic solvents. Firstly, 1.5 mL N-CDs solution with a certain concentration was added to the NC solution. After vigorously shaking to ensure homogenization, the excitation fluorescence spectra of these mixtures were recorded under the optimal emission wavelength at room temperature. Compared with detection in pure solvents, determining the content of NC in actual samples was more valuable. Energy drink, tea, and wine were purchased in the local market and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Then, different concentrations of NC were spiked into these authentic samples. After sonication for 30 min, 1.5 mL N-CDs solution and 1.5 mL authentic sample solution were mixed for fluorescence detection.

In order to evaluate the selectivity and interference of mental ions and other substances to N-CDs during the detection of NC, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs without NC was recorded under 500 µM ions and substances, including Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Na+, Zn2+, l-alanine, l-proline, l-serine, l-asparagine, Indigo, Quinoline yellow (QY), Amaranth (E 123) and Brilliant blue (BB). The fluorescence intensities were recorded under the optimal excitation.

To obtain the N-CDs/PVA nanocomposite, 1 g PVA was initially weighed and solubilized in 10 mL aqueous suspension of N-CDs. Subsequently, the mixture remained under a constant magnetic stirring for 1 h at a temperature of 90 °C and then was transferred to rectangular glass sheets for the water evaporation for approximately 48 h. Furthermore, the film was cut off into a rectangular sheet with 4 cm long and 2 cm wide to be applied in the solid-state lighting.

As seen in the TEM image in Fig. 1
(a), the as-prepared N-CDs are well dispersed and nearly uniform in size with diameters of approximately 2.8–4.2 nm (Fig. 1 (b)). The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of N-CDs (inset of Fig. 1 (a)) can be observed distinctly and the lattice spacing of N-CDs is determined to be 0.21 nm, which can be assigned to the (100) facet of graphite [19]. The morphological height of N-CDs is characterized by AFM. As shown in Fig. 1 (c), the distinguishable N-CDs represent uniform distribution. The average thickness of 0.84 nm indicates few graphite layers of carbon core. The Raman spectrum in Fig. S1 shows a disorder D band at 1365 cm−1 and a graphitic G band at 1569 cm−1 with an intensity ratio (ID/IG ) of 1.16. This value indicates a high crystallinity, which is in good accordance with the HR-TEM analysis. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1 (d)) displays a broad diffraction peak at 22.0° corresponding to a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, further validating the crystalline structure of N-CDs [20,21].

FT-IR and XPS are used to identify the chemical constituents and functional groups of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (e), the FT-IR spectra of melamine (purple line), OPD (red line) and N-CDs (green line) are compared. For melamine and OPD, the stretching vibration peaks of NH2/OH, CN and CN locate at 3123–3600 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1, respectively. For N-CDs, these peaks are also observed, indicating that N-CDs inherit the natural properties of raw materials during the synthesis process [22]. Moreover, the N-CDs exhibit sharper absorption peaks at 1498 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1, which confirm the presence of CO and CN bonds [23,24]. In the XPS full scan spectrum in Fig. 1 (f), three predominant peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s are presented at 284.1 eV, 399.1 eV and 532.1 eV, respectively. The C, N and O atomic concentrations are 53.7%, 42% and 4.3% ( Fig. S2 ), respectively. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 (g)) can be fitted into three components at 284.48 eV, 286.88 eV and 287.58 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO and CN/CO bonds, respectively [25,26]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 (h)) shows the existence of pyridinic N at 398.15 eV, pyrrolic N at 399.18 eV, amino N at 399.79 eV and graphitic N at 400.13 eV [27,28]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 (i)) displays two bonds at 531.28 eV and 532.28 eV, which further confirms the presence of the CO and CO bonds, respectively [29,30]. Taking all the results together, it can be safely concluded that N-CDs surface is rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing function groups, and N-CDs are successfully doped with N. The doping of N makes N-CDs possess novel fluorescence characteristics, and the abundant oxygen-containing groups ensure that the synthesized N-CDs have good water solubility, which are beneficial to the further effective detection of the target NC in various environments.

The optical properties of N-CDs are evaluated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy at room temperature. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs is recorded in Fig. 2
(a). The absorption peaks at 267 nm and 430 nm are assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains (CC) and n-π* transitions of surface states (nitrogen/oxygen-related states), respectively [31,32]. The fluorescence spectrum demonstrates that the emission of N-CDs occurs at 522 nm under the optimal excitation (459 nm). The N-CDs solution is brown under the sunlight and exhibits green fluorescence under 365 nm illumination. Moreover, the N-CDs exhibit an excitation-independent PL feature (Fig. 2 (b)). The 3D mapping of PL excitation and emission wavelength in Fig. 2 (c) displays two excitation centers, indicating that the same green fluorescence emission of N-CDs can be excited by two different excitation sources. Meanwhile, the FQY of N-CDs is calculated to be 32.6%. In addition, the fluorescence stability of N-CDs in different environments is investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 (d-e), the N-CDs perform tiny attenuation on PL intensity under ultraviolet light for 24 h and irradiation of visible light for 24 days, demonstrating the potent radiation resistance of N-CDs. Furthermore, the highly concentrated NaCl can impose ignorable influences on the PL intensity even when the concentration is up to 2 mol/L (Fig. 2 (f)). The above results indicate the high stability of N-CDs, which provides a solid foundation for potential applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation-induced emission and excitation behavior of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the concentration-dependent fluorescence property of N-CDs, the 2D fluorescence maps of N-CDs solution with different concentrations of 0.01 mg/mL, 0.07 mg/mL, 0.16 mg/mL, 0.25 mg/mL, 0.56 mg/mL and 0.83 mg/mL are performed in Fig. 3 (d-i). Fig. 3 (d) shows a symmetric ellipse fluorescence center within the excitation range from 405 to 455 nm. With the concentration increasing, the ellipse fluorescence center gradually becomes flat and elongated (0.07 mg/mL, Fig. 3 (e)). The maximum emission wavelength remains nearly constant at 519 nm, while the maximum excitation wavelength is broadened from 400 to 470 nm at the concentration of 0.16 mg/mL (Fig. 3 (f)). Interestingly, with the further increase of N-CDs concentration, two fluorescence centers are gradually separated (0.25 mg/mL and 0.56 mg/mL, Fig. 3 (g-h)). Finally, it splits into two excitation fluorescence centers of 373 nm and 474 nm (0.83 mg/mL, Fig. 3 (i)). These outcomes reveal that the concentration of N-CDs plays a crucial role in its fluorescence excitation behavior.

Subsequently, the excitation spectra of N-CDs with different concentrations are recorded to obtain more information about this bimodal excitation phenomenon (Fig. 4
(a)), which is the same as the 2D fluorescence matrix scans. When the concentration reaches the tipping point (0.25 mg/mL), the original broad peak splits into two peaks ( Fig. S6 ). By fitting the excitation spectra of N-CDs in Fig. S7 , it can be seen that all excitation spectra can always be fitted with two peaks (Ex.1 and Ex.2). As summarized in Table S1 , the Ex.1 gets away from the emission peak and Ex.2 gets close to the emission peak with the concentration increasing. The emission peak and Ex.2 generate the red shift simultaneously, but the shift rate of the emission peak is in arrears of that of the Ex.2. The distance between these two excitation peaks can be simply modulated by the concentration of N-CDs. Hence, it is of great potential to develop a ratiometric sensor based on this aggregation-induced bimodal excitation behavior.

The synthesis process of N-CDs is also investigated to identify the bimodal excitation origin. According to the FT-IR (Fig. 1 (e)) and XPS results (Fig. 1 (h-i)), as-synthesized N-CDs contain nitrogen dopants and are rich with nitrogen- and oxygen-containing surface functional groups, especially amine, hydroxyl (OH) and carboxylic acid (COOH). Generally, there is a strong relationship between the structural features and PLE properties of CDs. Thus, the bimodal excitation states on the surface of N-CDs may be linked to the coexistence of surface functional groups and N-doping in the CDs. It has been reported that introduced N and O atoms may create new energy levels and change the electric potential of N-CDs [28]. For example, Liao et al. noted that the nitrogen doping makes the sp2 carbon clusters rich in nitrogen-related states, providing the positive charge for the N-doping sp2 carbon cluster [37]. Wang et al. found weak electrostatic repulsion by decreasing the content of oxygen-containing functional groups (such as the carboxy group) and explained its role in electric potential of CDs [36]. Therefore, the nitrogen/oxygen-containing functional groups and structural configuration can induce the discrepancy of local electric potential in N-CDs, which will inevitably influence the excited electrons transfer. As shown in Fig. S8 , the absolute value of zeta potential gradually increases with the concentration increasing, which is attributed to the presence of abundant oxygen-containing surface functional groups of N-CDs. Thus, mutual repulsion occurs among the oxygen-related states of different positions, and the blue-shift phenomenon of short-wavelength excitation peak appears. On the contrary, nitrogen doping induces the formation of pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen in the sp2 carbon clusters and provides a positive charge. Consequently, the electrostatic attraction will cause a high local concentration of N-CDs monomers on the surface/borders, resulting in the red shift of the long-wavelength excitation peak [38]. Therefore, the local positions of N-CDs present different electric potentials, and the corresponding nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups will pull or push each other directionally during the aggregation process. As a result, oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups serve as the origin of the bimodal excitation centers of N-CDs, resulting in different PLE wavelength shifts in response to concentration changes, which is also consistent with our inference.

Other than that, the time-resolved measurements of N-CDs with different concentrations and excitation conditions are performed to reveal the kinetics of the deactivation of electrons from an excited state to the ground state. Fig. 4 (b) represents the fluorescence decay profile of N-CDs, which can be fitted with double exponential function:
(2)
I
t
=
A 1
e x p
- t / τ
1
+
A 2
e x p
- t / τ
2
where A1 and A2 are the fractional contributions to PL emission from two transition channels with corresponding to the decay time or lifetime τ1 (channel-1) and τ2 (channel-2). Double exponential decay lifetime values indicate that electrons have two distinct radiative transition channels [39]. Specifically, we speculate that channel-1 and channel-2 are attributed to the oxygen- and nitrogen-related states, which contain the radiative and non-radiative relaxation of electrons. Through fitting the lifetime of N-CDs under the short-wavelength excitation, the channel with τ1 ∼2.87 ns (channel-1) contributes to 71.0% of the PL emission weight, while the channel with τ2 ∼4.43 ns (channel-2) contributes to 29.0% weight. However, under the long-wavelength excitation, the reversal appears for the PL emission weight of τ1 and τ2 . Although there are two distinct radiative transition channels, it finally relaxes into the surface state energy levels no matter which energy level of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) band an electron is excited to [40]. Thus, it can explain that N-CDs exhibit the same emission wavelength under two different excitations. Besides, the average lifetime (τav ) is calculated using the following equation:
(3)
τ
av
=
∑ i
B
i
τ
i
2
∑ i
B i
τ i

The calculated τav of N-CDs under the two different excitations are 3.28 ns and 3.27 ns, respectively. Furthermore, accompanied with the concentration increasing from N-CDs-1 to N-CDs-4 (the obtained four different concentration N-CDs solutions are named as N-CDs-1 (0.16 mg/mL), N-CDs-2 (0.25 mg/mL), N-CDs-3 (0.37 mg/mL) and N-CDs-4 (0.83 mg/mL), respectively), the τav tends to shorten (Fig. 4 (c)). In addition, with the concentration increase, the long-wavelength excitation spectra of N-CDs show perceptible red shift and the lifetime of N-CDs at the concentrated condition is also prolonged from 3.18 to 5.82 ns ( Table S2 , τ2 ). These results further support our deduction above. Therefore, according to the quantum confinement effect, energy level splitting and broadening occur in the N-CDs aggregation process, which causes a reduction of the band gap [41]. Therefore, as the increase of N-CDs concentration, on the one hand, the Eg value declines gradually. On the other hand, the excited state energy levels within the n-π* gap also split and broaden ( Fig. S9 ), which generates the aggregation-induced bimodal excitation property of N-CDs. From these results, the schematic diagram of the intrinsic bimodal excitation mechanism of N-CDs is depicted in Fig. 4 (d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity experiment and detection of NC
ce:section.ce:para: By studying the fluorescence response of the N-CDs to NC and other interfering substances that may be present in the actual samples, such as common metal ions (Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Na+, Zn2+), substances (l-alanine, l-proline, l-serine, l-asparagine) and dyes (Indigo, QY, E 123, BB), the selectivity of N-CDs-based fluorescent nano-sensor is investigated. As depicted in Fig. S10 , it is evident that only NC can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs, while other possible interfering substances do not cause significant changes in the fluorescence of N-CDs, which indicates that the selectivity of N-CDs-based nano-sensor to NC is higher than other potential interferences.

Encouraged by the novel aggregation-induced bimodal excitation property of N-CDs, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the quantitative detection of NC is developed. As shown in Fig. 5
(a), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually drops with the increased concentration of NC, and the decline of the long-wavelength excitation is much greater than that of the short one. As displays in the Fig. S11 , the quenching reaction process enable to last about 140 s and then reach a complete state upon the addition of NC. Fig. 5 (b) depicts that the excitation intensity ratio of I459/I401 (I401 and I459 are the excitation intensities of N-CDs at 401 nm and 459 nm) decreases with the NC concentration ranging from 0 to 1000 μM. An excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9969) between the ratio of I459/I401 and the concentration of NC can be obtained in the range of 0–100 μM (Fig. 5 (b) inset). The fitted linear equation can be described as Y = -0.0010X + 1.0250 (where Y denotes the ratio of I459/I401 and X represents the concentration of NC). The LOD is determined to be as low as 32.1 nM based on 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve). Correspondingly, a noticeable color change from yellow to dark red can be observed via the naked eye, and a remarkable fluorescence quenching of the mixture occurs (Fig. 5 (c)).

Meanwhile, compared with the mono-signal-based detection under the different excitation ( Fig. S12 ), the detection limit of 32.1 nM from the ratiometric analysis is much lower than those (89.6 nM and 96.8 nM). In addition, the detection of NC in the different solvent environments is also carried out in Fig. S13 . It exhibits that the mono-signal-based detection gives a detection limit of 178 nM and 148 nM, respectively, whereas the ratiometric method similarly presents a lower LOD of 93.9 nM. The above results indicate that the bimodal excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence detection with a dual referential peaks system is superior to single peak fluorescence detection. More significantly, the entire detection process for NC can be completed rapidly in a straightforward operation process and does not need sophisticated pretreatment procedures. Table S3 summarizes current studies about utilizing laboratory instruments and CDs-based fluorescence materials for detecting NC, and the bimodal excitation N-CDs sensor in this work has much higher sensitivity and wider detection range, indicating that this innovative detection system can be applied as a sensitive ratiometric probe for NC detection in solution.

Subsequently, the sensing mechanism of N-CDs to NC is discussed. Fig. 5 (d) exhibits the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs (curve i), NC (curve ii), the mixtures of N-CDs and NC (curve iii) and the sum of curve i + curve ii (curve iv). It can be observed that curve iii completely overlaps with curve iv. This result indicates that the interaction between N-CDs and NC is fragile, and no new functional groups are formed or adhered to the surface of N-CDs [42]. In addition, by comparing the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NC with the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in Fig. 5 (e), the broad absorption peak at 518 nm of NC overlaps the excitation and emission peaks of N-CDs. This means that the synergistic effect of FRET and IFE may cause the fluorescence quenching phenomenon [43]. To demonstrate this hypothesis, the corresponding fluorescence lifetimes are measured. Fig. 5 (f) shows the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of N-CDs before and after adding NC, respectively. The average fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs in the absence and presence of NC are 3.27 ns and 3.08 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime changes slightly, suggesting that the IFE dominates the fluorescence quenching and FRET serves as a subordinated factor. Thus, the intrinsic fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs includes two parts: the competitive energy absorption between N-CDs and NC, and the transfer of emission energy from N-CDs to NC, which lead to not only fluorescence emission quenching (λem = 522 nm) but also a differentiated reduction in multi-excitation signals of the N-CDs in the presence of NC. From these results, the schematic diagram of the intrinsic detection mechanism of NC is depicted in Fig. 5 (g). To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time using an IFE-based bimodal excitation ratiometric fluorescence sensor to detect NC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of NC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Given its wide range in foodstuffs and potential genotoxic, extensive or long-term exposure to NC will be detrimental to organisms. Consequently, the determination of NC in actual samples is of great significance. To validate the practicability of the bimodal excitation fluorescence ratiometric sensor, several concentrations of NC are spiked in the energy drink, tea, and wine for detection. Meanwhile, the detection of NC is also implemented by HPLC as a reference method [44]. The analytical results are shown in Table 1 . The percentage recoveries are within the rational range of 92.8–110.8%, and the corresponding relative standard deviations in percentage (%RSD) are lower than 4.46. Moreover, the t-test is investigated, showing no significant differences between the two methods at a 95% confidence level, indicating that our proposed method has excellent accuracy and reliability. The outcomes further reveal that our developed sensor has a significant potential for detecting NC residues with high selectivity and sensitivity in actual food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor film based on aggregation-induced multicolor of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Given the excellent aggregation-induced tunable multicolor effect, an N-CDs-based nanocomposite is prepared using PVA films as a polymeric matrix and several different concentration aqueous suspensions of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6
(a), the fluorescent N-CDs are successfully immobilized onto polyamide film. The four prepared films containing different concentrations of N-CDs are named film-1 (0.16 mg/mL), film-2 (0.37 mg/mL), film-3 (1.12 mg/mL) and film-4 (1.87 mg/mL), respectively. With the increase of the N-CDs concentration, the fluorescence of N-CDs films changes from blue to yellow under UV light irradiation, as depicted in Fig. 6 (b). Fig. 6 (c) shows the normalized fluorescence emission spectra of the obtained films, in which the maximum emission peak shifts gradually from 519 to 572 nm under optimal excitation wavelengths. Meanwhile, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the fluorescent films are recorded in Fig. 6 (d), and the bandgaps are calculated in Fig. 6 (e). It is evident that as the concentration increases, the energy gap gradually reduces, which agrees well with the redshift of PL emission. The above results are similar to the corresponding optical properties of N-CDs in solution, confirming that PVA molecules have a negligible effect on their fluorescence characteristics. Moreover, PVA acts as a surface passivation agent that prevents the interaction of N-CDs with the external environment [45]. Thus, the concentration-modulated multicolor features of the N-CDs in the solution state can be extended to solid-state polymer matrices, giving N-CDs great potential in solid-state lighting applications.

In conclusion, the prepared N-CDs exhibit an innovative aggregation-induced bimodal excitation fluorescence behavior, resulting from energy level splitting and broadening, which have been demonstrated by investigating the kinetic processes of electrons and the discrepancy of electric potential in N-CDs. Upon the addition of NC, due to the different degree overlap between the NC broad absorption peak at 518 nm and two excitation peaks, it leads to a differentiated reduction in multi-excitation signals of N-CDs. Thus, based on this novel phenomenon, a dual-excitation ratiometric fluorescence sensor for NC detection is developed successfully. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I459/I401 reveals an excellent linear relationship with NC concentrations from 0 to 100 µM (R2 = 0.9969), and the LOD is 32.1 nM. Besides, we also achieve solid-state lighting films by incorporating N-CDs into PVA films. The immobilization of N-CDs in the PVA matrix can stabilize the fluorescence of N-CDs by inhibiting the aggregation of N-CDs. Overall, this work provides a new view of the innovative aggregation-induced bimodal excitation fluorescence behavior and further broadens their application for ratiometric fluorescence sensors and solid-state lighting.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.04.114.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
